data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse resulted from an abrupt socio-technical phase transition when Linear B administrative systems lost asymmetric information advantages over competing memory institutions","mechanism": "Palace economies depended on scribal monopolies controlling information asymmetry; the diffusion of alternative accounting practices and merchant alphabets eroded this monopoly, triggering coordination failure cascades across networked polities","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Mesopotamia", "Cyprus"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Discovery of Linear B tablets post-dating 1150 BC demonstrating continued palace administration in Crete or the mainland", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological evidence that Ugarit abandoned alphabetic writing and returned to cuneiform-only administration before destruction", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian New Kingdom papyri showing similar scribal-monopoly crises in Nile delta administrative centers without systemic collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Inverts materialist explanations by privileging information-structure; atypical in treating Egypt's survival as requiring separate explanation rather than treating exclusion as theoretically motivated."}
